---
title: "embedpdf Example"
resources:
  - dummy.pdf
  - dummy2.pdf
---

## Simple Usage

All you really need to give the `pdf` shortcode is the path to the PDF file you want to embed in your Quarto HTML document.

`{{{< pdf dummy.pdf >}}}`

{{< pdf dummy.pdf >}}


## Customization

However, in most cases, you will want to customize the appearance of your embedded PDF object. I have built a few common HTML-based customizations into the `pdf` shortcode for your convenience. However, the most powerful and flexible approach to customization will be to use cascading style sheets or CSS (e.g., by passing the `pdf` shortcode a CSS class). If you are new to CSS, there are some good introductory resources available here and here.


### Embed with width in pixels

`{{{< pdf dummy.pdf width=600 >}}}`

{{< pdf dummy.pdf width=600 >}}


### Embed with width in percent

`{{{< pdf dummy.pdf width=100% >}}}`

{{< pdf dummy.pdf width=100% >}}


### Embed with height in pixels

`{{{< pdf dummy.pdf height=600 >}}}`

{{< pdf dummy.pdf height=600 >}}


### Embed with both width and height

`{{{< pdf dummy.pdf width=600 height=400 >}}}`

{{< pdf dummy.pdf width=600 height=400 >}}


### Embed with 1 px border

`{{{< pdf dummy.pdf border=1 >}}}`

{{< pdf dummy.pdf border=1 >}}


### Embed with CSS class

`{{{< pdf dummy.pdf class=myclass >}}}`

{{< pdf dummy.pdf class=myclass >}}

### Add download button

`{{{< pdf dummy.pdf button="Download PDF" >}}}`

{{< pdf dummy.pdf button="Download PDF" >}}

## Supporting Mobile Browsers

Mobile browsers (e.g., Chrome and Firefox on Android) do not support the inline rendering of PDF objects: they show a blank box or a download prompt instead. To solve this, the extension now includes a JavaScript-based PDF renderer ([PDF.js](https://mozilla.github.io/pdf.js/)).

By default (`renderer=auto`), each embedded PDF checks whether the browser can display PDFs inline (via `navigator.pdfViewerEnabled`). If it can, the browser's native PDF viewer is used, exactly as before. If it cannot (e.g., on mobile), the PDF is instead drawn page-by-page onto the screen by PDF.js, with a small toolbar for zooming, page tracking, and downloading. Desktop visitors never download the PDF.js library; it is only fetched when needed.

### Force the PDF.js renderer

You can force the PDF.js renderer with `renderer=pdfjs`, which is also handy for previewing on a desktop what mobile visitors will see:

`{{{< pdf dummy2.pdf renderer=pdfjs height=500 >}}}`

{{< pdf dummy2.pdf renderer=pdfjs height=500 >}}

### Force the native renderer

Similarly, `renderer=native` always uses the browser's built-in viewer (e.g., for self-contained documents using `embed-resources: true`, where PDF.js cannot fetch the PDF file):

`{{{< pdf dummy.pdf renderer=native height=400 >}}}`

{{< pdf dummy.pdf renderer=native height=400 >}}

### Hide the PDF.js toolbar

`{{{< pdf dummy2.pdf renderer=pdfjs toolbar=false height=400 >}}}`

{{< pdf dummy2.pdf renderer=pdfjs toolbar=false height=400 >}}

### Setting defaults for the whole document or project

You can set default values for any shortcode option under the `embedpdf` key in your document YAML header or `_quarto.yml`:

```yaml
embedpdf:
  renderer: pdfjs
  height: 600
```

## Fallback Options

### Add a backup image

If JavaScript is disabled, the visitor instead sees the provided image with a hyperlink to download the PDF file. I usually take a screenshot of the PDF file to create this image, but you could use any image you like (e.g., a download button image). For this reason, I provide separate customization arguments for the image.

`{{{< pdf dummy.pdf width=400 height=600 border=1 image=dummy.png image_width=400 image_border=1 >}}}`

{{< pdf dummy.pdf width=400 height=600 border=1 image=dummy.png image_width=400 image_border=1 >}}

To see what this will look like on a browser that does support PDF (e.g., during development), you can use `image_force=TRUE`:

`{{{< pdf dummy.pdf width=400 height=600 image=dummy.png image_force=TRUE border=1 image_width=400 image_border=1 >}}}`

{{< pdf dummy.pdf width=400 height=600 image=dummy.png image_force=TRUE border=1 image_width=400 image_border=1 >}}

The button will work on mobile browsers, so that's another option that you can use in addition to or in place of the backup image.

`{{{< pdf dummy.pdf button="Download" width=400 height=600 image=dummy.png image_force=TRUE border=1 image_width=400 image_border=1 >}}}`

{{< pdf dummy.pdf button="Download" width=400 height=600 image=dummy.png image_force=TRUE border=1 image_width=400 image_border=1 >}}
